pattern: Greedily dragging the smallest reachable digit to the front is optimal, and tracking how many not-yet-placed digits still sit in front of a candidate -- rather than its raw original index -- turns each drag's swap cost into a simple prefix-count query.
hints: Process the answer left to right: at each output position, you want the smallest digit that is close enough (in swap cost) to reach the front given your remaining budget.

The cost to bring a not-yet-used digit to the current front equals the number of not-yet-used digits that currently sit before it -- not its original string index, since digits already placed no longer count.

Keep, per digit value, a queue of its remaining original positions, and use a Fenwick tree over 'still unused' flags to get that before-count in O(log n); after choosing a digit, remove it from the tree and subtract its cost from the budget.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, leading zero allowed in the output
statement example, budget far exceeds what's needed
single digit, any budget leaves it unchanged
fully descending digits with enough budget to fully sort ascending
exactly one swap needed and exactly one allowed
all identical digits, budget is irrelevant
budget covers only part of the needed drag distance

stress.description: a longer random digit string with a moderate swap budget, deterministic seed
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1500))
    digits = [str(rng.randint(0, 9)) for _ in range(n)]
    if n > 1 and digits[0] == '0':
        digits[0] = '1'
    num = ''.join(digits)
    k = rng.randint(1, n * 5 + 1)
    return [num, k]
stress.note: n kept modest so the O(n^2) reference-check oracle (a direct swap simulation) stays fast; the optimized solution itself scales to the full 3e4 constraint
